title: Cat Swallowed String: Emergency Guide & Triage Steps
meta_desc: If your cat swallowed string, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn about linear foreign bodies and why you should never pull on the string.
content_html:
TL;DR: Swallowing string is a life-threatening emergency for cats that requires immediate veterinary attention; never attempt to pull the string out yourself as it can cause fatal internal damage.

What is a linear foreign body and how does it affect my cat?

When a cat or kitten swallows a piece of string, yarn, or dental floss, it is medically classified as a linear foreign body. Unlike a solid object, string can become anchored at one point—often under the tongue or at the stomach exit—while the rest of the material attempts to move through the digestive tract. This creates a dangerous 'sawing' effect, causing the intestines to bunch up or even tear open.

Is it a medical emergency if my cat swallows string?

  • Yes, this is considered a life-threatening emergency because internal damage can progress rapidly even if your cat appears normal for several hours.
  • It is critical that you do not attempt to pull on any string you see protruding from your cat's mouth or anus, as this can cause fatal damage to the intestinal lining.
  • Contact an emergency veterinarian immediately for professional assessment.

How does providing a photo or video help with veterinary triage?

  • Taking a photo of the type of string swallowed, or any remaining pieces, helps the vet determine the texture, potential length, and likelihood of the material getting caught.
  • If your cat is vomiting or gagging, a video of these behaviors helps the medical team assess the severity of the situation before you even arrive at the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cases of feline linear foreign bodies, such as thread or yarn, exploratory laparotomy is indicated if the foreign body is distal to the pyloric region, if there are foreign bodies at multiple locations, or if there are signs of septic peritonitis. During surgery, the entire gastrointestinal tract must be inspected for objects that could cause obstruction, and the vitality of the GI tract must be assessed; devitalized or perforated areas must be resected. Gentle manipulation may free the foreign body from distal attachments, allowing removal through a gastrotomy; otherwise, multiple enterotomies may be necessary. Radiographs should be taken before recovery from anesthesia to exclude the presence of multiple foreign bodies.

Chapter: Surgery, Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 392)

faqs.question: Should I wait to see if the string passes in the stool?
faqs.answer: No. Waiting is extremely risky. Because string often becomes anchored internally, it rarely passes on its own and can cause the intestines to fold in on themselves, leading to a surgical emergency.

faqs.question: Can I pull the string out if I see it under the tongue?
faqs.answer: Absolutely not. Pulling on string that is caught deep in the digestive tract can act like a knife, cutting through the intestines. Only a veterinarian should remove the material.

faqs.question: What are the common symptoms of a string blockage?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include vomiting, loss of appetite, lethargy, abdominal pain, or hiding. However, symptoms may not appear immediately, which is why early intervention is vital.
